chain_of_thought: 1. CRASH SITE ANALYSIS: The crash occurs in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum at 0x0011dcc0. This function iterates over ASN1 structure children by reading bytes from a data buffer (param_1 + 0x10) and computing lengths. The loop condition is: local_28 != iVar3 (where iVar3 = Compute_ASN1_ValueLength(param_1)).

2. VULNERABILITY IN Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum: The function reads byte data from *(long*)(param_1 + 0x10) using local_28 as an index. The loop increments local_28 by (1 + local_30 + local_34) each iteration, where local_30 and local_34 come from Compute_ASN1_LengthSize and Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO. The loop exit condition is local_28 == iVar3 (exact equality). If the computed length+size values cause local_28 to SKIP PAST iVar3 (overshoot), the loop will never terminate with the equality check and will read out of bounds. This is a classic off-by-skip OOB read.

3. DETAIL ON THE OOB: The loop updates local_28 at the top: local_28 = local_28 + local_30 + local_34. If this sum exceeds iVar3, the != check won't catch it and the loop continues reading past the buffer boundary. While Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO returns -1 for some error cases (which is caught), a crafted ASN1 length field can cause the sum to skip over iVar3 without equaling it.

4. INTEGER OVERFLOW IN Compute_ASN1_LengthSize: The function returns (*param_1 & 0x7f) + 1 when high bit is set. This can return values up to 128. If param_1[0] is 0xFF, it returns 0x80 (128). The subsequent Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO reads that many bytes, which can exceed the actual buffer allocated in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.

5. DATA FLOW FROM JNI: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID receives a byte[] from Java (param_3). It calls GetArrayLength (JNI offset 0x558) to get size, GetByteArrayElements (offset 0x5c0) to get data. The data is copied to a malloc'd buffer and passed to BINSTR_SetData, then CERT_Load. CERT_Load calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST which parses the raw bytes into an ASN1 structure.

6. Decode_DER_ASN1_ST VULNERABILITY: This function reads the length from param_1[1]. If the high bit is set, it reads (param_1[1] & 0x7f) more bytes for the extended length. It then computes local_34 from these bytes and calls malloc(local_34) and memcpy(data, param_1 + offset, local_34). The memcpy reads local_34 bytes from param_1 starting at offset (local_30 + 2). If local_34 is crafted to be larger than the actual available data, this is a heap buffer over-read. The only check is (int)local_34 < 0, which only catches negative values but not values exceeding the actual input buffer size.

7. ATTACKER CONTROL: The byte[] array is directly passed from Java to native. The Java call graph shows multiple paths reaching jGetPolicyOID from com.quram.mi.ocr classes. The byte array content is fully attacker-controlled - it represents certificate data that could come from network or file input. A malformed ASN1/DER certificate with crafted length fields will trigger the OOB read in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum or Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.

8. MISSING BOUNDS CHECKS: Neither Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum nor Decode_DER_ASN1_ST validates that the computed lengths don't exceed the actual buffer size. The ASN1 structures store data pointers and length metadata but there's no total-buffer-size field to validate against.

9. CONCLUSION: This is a genuine vulnerability. A crafted DER-encoded certificate with inconsistent/malicious length fields can cause out-of-bounds memory reads in the ASN1 parsing code. The input is attacker-controlled byte[] data passed through JNI. The vulnerability is in the certificate parsing library (libUbikeyJni.so) which lacks proper bounds validation during ASN1/DER decoding.
reasons: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum uses equality check (!=) for loop termination which can be overshot by crafted ASN1 length fields, causing OOB read
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST reads length from attacker-controlled bytes and performs memcpy without validating against actual input buffer size
No bounds checking between computed ASN1 value lengths and actual allocated buffer sizes throughout the parsing chain
Input byte[] is directly attacker-controlled via JNI bridge from Java, representing certificate data that can be malformed

evidence.snippet: for (local_28 = 0; local_28 = local_28 + local_30 + local_34, local_28 != iVar3; local_28 = local_28 + 1) { bVar2 = *(byte *)(*(long *)(param_1 + 0x10) + (long)local_28); }
evidence.note: Loop termination uses != (equality) check. If local_28 + local_30 + local_34 + 1 overshoots iVar3, loop continues reading OOB.
local_34 is computed from attacker-controlled length bytes in DER input. Only checks (int)local_34 < 0, not whether it exceeds actual input size.
Raw attacker-controlled byte[] from Java is passed directly to CERT_Load without any structural validation.
Returns up to 128 for extended length encoding. No validation that this many bytes are actually available in the buffer.

recommendations: Add total buffer size tracking to ASN1 structures and validate all computed lengths against remaining buffer size
Replace the != equality loop termination in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum with >= to prevent overshoot
Add input validation/sanity checks on DER-encoded certificate data before parsing (e.g., minimum/maximum size, structural pre-validation)
assumptions: The fuzzer provides arbitrary byte arrays that simulate malformed DER-encoded certificates
The obfuscated Java classes (com.quram.mi.ocr.*) pass certificate byte data that could originate from network or file sources
Both copies of functions (thunk wrappers at 0x0011xxxx and real implementations at 0x0015xxxx/0x0011dxxx/0x0012xxxx) are in the same library
limitations: Could not resolve obfuscated Java caller classes (com.quram.mi.ocr.r14, zo1, h63) to verify exact data origin
Exact crash trigger (which specific malformed field) is not known from the stack trace alone
Cannot determine if the app performs any certificate format pre-validation in Java before calling JNI

exploit.trigger_method: Supply a crafted DER-encoded certificate via the jGetPolicyOID JNI method with malicious ASN1 length fields that cause Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum to read past the allocated buffer
prerequisites: Ability to supply arbitrary byte[] data to UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID() - achievable by providing a malformed certificate file/blob to the OCR/certificate processing flow
The app processes certificate data from external sources (network, file, or IPC)
exploit_pipeline: 1. Craft a malformed DER-encoded X.509 certificate where inner TBS Certificate SEQUENCE has ASN1 length fields that cause the child-counting loop to overshoot the buffer boundary
2. Deliver the crafted certificate to the app via a mechanism that reaches the OCR/certificate pipeline (e.g., push file to device storage or intercept network certificate exchange)
3. The byte[] is passed through JNI to jGetPolicyOID which calls CERT_Load -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum
4. Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum reads past the heap buffer due to the != loop condition and crafted lengths, leaking heap memory or crashing the process
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n kr.co.busanbank.mbp/.a --es cert_file /sdcard/Download/payload.der
exploit.notes: The crafted certificate needs a valid outer SEQUENCE tag but inner TBS Certificate content with inconsistent length fields. Specifically, child elements whose tag+length+value sizes sum to a value that skips over the total declared length without equality, causing unbounded iteration.

chain_of_thought: Step 1: Identify the crash site. The crash is in __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. The stack trace shows: __memcpy -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Get_DER_Child -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID.

Step 2: Analyze the JNI entry point (Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID at 0x001178bc). It receives a byte array from Java (param_3). It calls GetArrayLength to get __size, GetByteArrayElements to get __src, then malloc(__size) and memcpy(__s, __src, __size). Then it creates a BINSTR with BINSTR_SetData(__s, iVar2, auStack_438) and calls CERT_Load(&local_440, auStack_438). No validation of the byte array content is performed—raw bytes are passed directly to the DER parser.

Step 3: Analyze CERT_Load at 0x001369a4. It calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST(*param_2) where *param_2 is the raw byte data from the Java byte array. Then calls Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT on the decoded ASN1 structure.

Step 4: Analyze Decode_DER_ASN1_ST at 0x001221d0 (the real implementation, not the thunk). This is the CRITICAL function where the crash occurs. It parses DER-encoded ASN.1 data: (a) reads tag byte from param_1[0], (b) reads length byte from param_1[1], (c) if high bit set, interprets lower 7 bits as number of following length bytes (local_30 = param_1[1] & 0x7f), (d) computes local_34 as the content length from those bytes, (e) then does: memcpy(local_40[2], param_1 + (local_30 + 2), (long)(int)local_34).

Step 5: Identify the vulnerability. The key issue is in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST: local_34 is computed from the length bytes embedded in the DER data (attacker-controlled). The memcpy copies local_34 bytes from param_1 + (local_30 + 2). However, param_1 points to the raw certificate data from the Java byte array, and local_34 is derived from the DER length field within that data. There is NO bounds check to ensure local_34 does not exceed the actual size of the input buffer. A crafted certificate with a length field claiming more data than actually present will cause memcpy to read beyond the buffer boundary.

Step 6: The malloc((long)(int)local_34) allocates the destination buffer based on the claimed length, so the destination is large enough. But the SOURCE (param_1 + local_30 + 2) may have far fewer bytes available than local_34. This is a classic out-of-bounds read vulnerability—memcpy reads past the end of the input buffer.

Step 7: Verify attacker control. The byte array passed to jGetPolicyOID comes from Java. Looking at the Java call graph: com.quram.mi.ocr.zo1.j(byte[]) -> ... -> UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID(byte[]). The zo1 class method j() simply calls t.jGetPolicyOID(bArr) with no validation. The byte array is passed through from the caller. In a banking app context, certificates could come from network/file sources.

Step 8: Additional vulnerability in Get_DER_Child: This function iterates over ASN1 children using Compute_ASN1_ValueLength as the loop bound, but individual child lengths are parsed from the data itself. If a child's length field is crafted to be larger than the actual content, access to *(long *)(param_1 + 0x10) + (long)local_30 could go out of bounds.

Step 9: Confirm this is not a harness issue. The vulnerability is in the DER parsing logic itself—it trusts length fields from the input without validating them against the actual buffer size. This is a genuine code vulnerability, not a harness artifact. Any malformed certificate data triggers this.

Step 10: The check 'if ((int)local_34 < 0)' only rejects negative values (when cast to int), but does not check if local_34 exceeds the actual input buffer size. The actual input size is never tracked or compared against.
reasons: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST reads DER length fields from attacker-controlled input and uses them directly as the size argument to memcpy without validating against the actual input buffer size
The memcpy source (param_1 + local_30 + 2) can extend beyond the actual certificate byte array if the DER length field is crafted to be larger than the real data
No bounds checking exists between the parsed DER length and the actual buffer size anywhere in the call chain from JNI entry to the crash site
Java side passes raw byte array directly to native code with no content validation

evidence.note: local_34 is derived from DER length bytes in attacker-controlled input; no check that local_34 + local_30 + 2 <= actual input buffer size

evidence.snippet: memcpy(__s, __src, __size); ... BINSTR_SetData(__s, iVar2, auStack_438); CERT_Load(&local_440, auStack_438);
evidence.note: Raw byte array from Java passed through to DER parser with no content validation
Directly calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST on attacker-controlled certificate data
Java wrapper passes byte array directly to JNI with no validation

recommendations: Add input buffer size tracking: pass the actual buffer length alongside the data pointer into Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and validate that local_34 + local_30 + 2 does not exceed the buffer size before calling memcpy
Validate DER structure integrity before deep parsing: check that all length fields are consistent with the total input size
Consider using a well-tested ASN.1 parsing library with built-in bounds checking instead of custom DER parsing
assumptions: The byte array passed to jGetPolicyOID can contain attacker-influenced data (e.g., certificates received from network, files, or IPC in the banking app context)
The Decode_DER_ASN1_ST at 0x001221d0 is the real implementation called through the thunk at 0x001107b0
limitations: Could not resolve obfuscated Java call graph classes (com.quram.mi.ocr.r14, com.quram.mi.ocr.zo1, com.quram.mi.ocr.h63) in Jadx to determine the ultimate source of the byte array
Some indirect function calls in Get_DER_Child and Decode_DER_ASN1_STRING could not be fully resolved

exploit.trigger_method: Supply a crafted DER-encoded certificate byte array with an inflated length field to jGetPolicyOID, causing memcpy to read beyond the allocated buffer
prerequisites: Ability to provide a malformed certificate byte array that reaches jGetPolicyOID (e.g., via OCR processing path or certificate import)
The app processes external certificate data through the com.quram.mi.ocr path
exploit_pipeline: 1. Craft a minimal DER-encoded blob where the top-level SEQUENCE length field claims N bytes of content, but only M < N bytes are actually present
2. Deliver the crafted blob to the app through any path that reaches jGetPolicyOID (e.g., OCR processing, certificate import)
3. The native code will malloc(N) for the destination, then memcpy N bytes from the M-byte source buffer, reading N-M bytes out of bounds
4. Depending on heap layout, this leaks adjacent heap data (info disclosure) or triggers a crash/heap corruption
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n kr.co.busanbank.mbp/.a --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der
exploit.notes: The DER blob uses a 4-byte length encoding (0x84 prefix) claiming 0x10000 bytes but provides only 16 bytes. This forces memcpy to read ~65520 bytes beyond the buffer. In practice, the exact delivery mechanism depends on which app component feeds data into the OCR/certificate path.
